flashcards.front: What is the root interface of the entire Java Collections Framework hierarchy?
flashcards.back: The Collection interface is the root interface. It is part of java.util package and extends Iterable. It does NOT include Map - Map is a separate hierarchy.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between List, Set, and Queue interfaces?
flashcards.back: List: Ordered, allows duplicates, indexed access (ArrayList, LinkedList, Vector). Set: Unordered, no duplicates (HashSet, TreeSet, LinkedHashSet). Queue: FIFO or priority-based, typically allows duplicates (PriorityQueue, ArrayDeque).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList?
flashcards.back: ArrayList: O(1) random access, O(n) insertion/deletion (shifting). LinkedList: O(1) insertion/deletion at ends, O(n) random access. ArrayList is better for frequent reads; LinkedList for frequent insertions/deletions.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between HashSet, LinkedHashSet, and TreeSet?
flashcards.back: HashSet: Unordered, O(1) add/remove/contains. LinkedHashSet: Maintains insertion order, O(1) operations. TreeSet: Sorted order (Red-Black tree), O(log n) operations, implements NavigableSet.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between HashMap, LinkedHashMap, and TreeMap?
flashcards.back: HashMap: Unordered, allows one null key and multiple null values. LinkedHashMap: Maintains insertion order. TreeMap: Sorted by keys (Red-Black tree), implements NavigableMap, no null keys allowed.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the Iterator interface?
flashcards.back: Iterator provides a universal way to traverse collections. It has methods: hasNext(), next(), remove(). It's safer than Enumeration (allows removal during iteration) and works with enhanced for-loop.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Iterator and ListIterator?
flashcards.back: Iterator: Unidirectional (forward only). ListIterator: Bidirectional (forward and backward), allows modification, knows index position. Only available for List implementations.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Comparable and Comparator?
flashcards.back: Comparable: Interface implemented by the class itself (natural ordering). One implementation per class. Comparator: External comparison logic. Multiple comparators possible. Used by TreeSet, TreeMap, Collections.sort().

flashcards.front: What are the Legacy classes in Collections Framework?
flashcards.back: Vector (synchronized List), Stack (extends Vector), Hashtable (synchronized Map), Enumeration (legacy iterator). All are synchronized and considered legacy since Java 1.2.

flashcards.front: What is the RandomAccess marker interface used for?
flashcards.back: It indicates the list supports fast (constant time) random access. ArrayList implements it; LinkedList does not. Used by algorithms to choose between iterative(index-based) or iterator-based traversal for optimal performance.

flashcards.front: What is the default initial capacity and load factor of HashMap?
flashcards.back: Default initial capacity is 16. Default load factor is 0.75. When size exceeds capacity × load factor, the HashMap doubles in size (capacity × 2) and all entries are rehashed.

flashcards.front: What are the core collection interfaces in order of inheritance?
flashcards.back: Iterable → Collection → (List, Set, Queue, Deque). Map is separate (extends Iterable). List → (ArrayList, LinkedList, Vector, Stack). Set → (HashSet, TreeSet, LinkedHashSet). Queue → (PriorityQueue, ArrayDeque). Map → (HashMap, TreeMap, LinkedHashMap, Hashtable).
